ground_truth.title: AI Vibe Coding, Is It Working? No
ground_truth.author: Dakara
ground_truth.main_content: AI Vibe Coding, Is It Working? No - Notes From the Desk: No. 53 - 2025.12.18. The article discusses Brian Jenney's experience attempting professional AI vibe coding at his company. Initially, the prototype looked impressive and felt magical, but when moving from demo to production, it became clear the approach was not viable. The author describes how AI made the team slower, not faster, despite expectations. The author also shares personal experiments: successfully using Gemini 3 Flash to create a Golang file that parses TTML files, but failing to create a working Zig application with multiple AI models. AI is characterized as an imperfect simulator of data patterns, working well only for common tasks with abundant training data, but failing for niche languages or problems. This creates conformity bias toward popular technologies and disincentivizes innovation with new tools. The article concludes that AI can unreliably automate common tasks, but this cannot justify the massive investments being made.
with: There are no doubt many grifters in this space, but also many true believers, due to AI's impressive mirage of capability.
When we went from prototype into something in production, it became immediately clear that this was not going to work.
AI made us slower, not faster, contrary to the productivity myth.
AI has no self-reflection. It does not know what it is good at. It cannot perceive its own knowledge and capabilities.
This incentivizes locking us into conformity with whatever is the most popular.

items.alt: Vibe Coding: Is AI ready to take over?
